01 / 04 Fixed-Wing Aircraft Design

Summer
Plane

A complete fixed-wing RC aircraft designed, built, and flown over the summer. The walkthrough below follows the project from digital model through laser-cut structure, electronics integration, and flight testing.

Complete
OpenVSP
Laser-Cut Structure
RC Fixed-Wing
Flight Tested
OpenVSP model
00

OpenVSP

A low fidelity 3D model of the plane completed in OpenVSP. To simplify the design process, the complex fuselage geometry was neglected and aerodynamic analysis was done exclusively on the flow over the wings, tail, and vertical stabilizer. OpenVSP was used repeatedly throughout the design process to test different flow configurations and obtain stability derivatives. Tuning the stability derivatives was a necessary process to ensure static stability, favorable dynamic behavior, and controls implementation.

Laser-cut parts — Fusion 360
01

Laser-Cut Structure

All the pieces for the fuselage and airfoil sections were made in Fusion 360 as 2D vector files. The laser-cut sections ensured that the wing shape was an accurate construction of the model. Using wood for the fuselage was not optimal, as it resulted in a heavier build than desired, and foam would have worked better. However, keeping the construction method the same helped streamline the manufacturing process, and this was not a large concern at the time.

Wing assembly — left wing
02

Wing Assembly

This photo shows the construction of the left wing, with two carbon fiber rods acting as spars to bring the pieces together. A total of 6 ribs and 2 spars were used for each wing, which left a convenient space for the ailerons to fit between rib sections.

Tail assembly — unskinned
03

Tail Assembly

This photo shows the horizontal tail and elevator in place at the rear of the fuselage, without skin. The string running down the fuselage length was used to actuate the elevator. Similar to the wings, both the tail and elevator used two spars.

Finished right wing
04

Finished Wing

The completed right wing, with the aileron in place, highlighted by the use of blue painter's tape contrasting the packing tape used on the rest of the wing. The servo is in place behind the aileron, with the wires running through each rib to access the central electronics system in the fuselage.

aileron demo
05

Aileron Demo

ailerons test
05b

Ailerons Test

Fuselage interior — IMU & elevator servo
06

Fuselage Interior

An early shot of the fuselage interior. Shown is the servo dedicated for elevator control, along with the inertial measurement unit, which was mounted with screws to keep it level with the plane.

moving CG forward
07

Moving CG Forward

Moments of inertia measurement rig
08

Weight & Balance

This is an image of my rudimentary setup meant to measure the plane's moments of inertia about the roll and pitch axes. I used leftover carbon fiber rods, string, and tape to create a cradle that could be used to swing the plane like a pendulum.

moments of inertia
08b

Moments of Inertia

Finished tail assembly
09

Finished Tail

The finished tail assembly, with packing and painter's tape covering the structural elements. The vertical stabilizer, previously unseen, is also assembled. A rudder was constructed in case trimming would be desired later on.

Completed airframe
10

Completed Airframe

A photo of the completed plane outside, with all electronics in place.

side view of plane
11

Side View of Plane

Post flight — broken prop
12

Post Flight

The plane's broken propeller from the final nosedive.

test flight 1
13

Test Flight 1

test flight 2
14

Test Flight 2

In this video you can see the ailerons work for a few seconds to stabilize the rolling effect on the plane. Unfortunately by this time, the vertical stabilizer had already broken off so the plane was vulnerable to sideslip.

Project Summary

Construction & Controls

Overall, I was pleased with the wing construction and the ailerons themselves. Their build was sturdy and reliable and I felt the roll control algorithm was effective, based on the videos shown. The pitch control algorithm also technically worked well — unfortunately early testing damage to the tail disabled the elevator so this effect is not shown. The tail (and vertical stabilizer) would have been improved if they were scaled up at the current design, or constructed out of foam at their current scale. The laser-cut sections were quite small, and getting the pieces to fit together cleanly and have the elevator actuate were both troublesome.

Control Algorithm

Both the roll and pitch control algorithms were written in Python on Thonny IDE, and ran based on joint data from the IMU. Accelerometer and gyroscope data were both passed through a low-pass filter to reduce noise, and then used at different proportions to dictate control surface movement. The smoothest motion came from using a 95/5 mix of the data, heavily biased towards the gyroscope feed. The accelerometer data was used on a slightly longer time scale to correct for gyroscope drift.

Weight & Lessons Learned

The plane was definitely too heavy, and the motor and prop assembly undersized for the job. Sourcing more components was outside the scope of the project, as all electronics were obtained for free from my school lab. The weight issue was further exacerbated by the addition of ballast at the nose — necessary to position the CG to match the stability calculations. The coins added at the nose contributed roughly 325g to a plane that was originally about 400g. This wasn't a huge concern as the primary goal was the stability and control aspect, and glide tests were sufficient to analyze that. However, she did stay in the air about as long as a brick.
